summarization.a: shows ZrO₂ thickness measured during repeated ALE cycles at 325 °C. WF₆/BCl₃ removes ZrO₂ fastest (film gone in ~3 cycles), WF₆/TiCl₄ is slightly slower (~4–5 cycles), and WF₆/SOCl₂ is ineffective (film persists after 25 cycles). The curves demonstrate self-limiting removal for the effective chemistries and a clear chemistry-dependent rate.
summarization.b: present AES composition measured before etching and after 5 and 25 cycles for each chemistry. AES corroborates ellipsometry: for BCl₃, Zr and O drop rapidly and B residues appear; for SOCl₂, S signal is detectable and Zr/O remain high (sulfate formation and poor removal); for TiCl₄, Zr decreases moderately while Cl/W signatures indicate incomplete volatile conversion. Together the panels link kinetics (how quickly ZrO₂ is removed) with surface chemistry (what residues form), explaining why BCl₃ is optimal for ZrO₂ ALE and SOCl₂ fails.
summarization.c: present AES composition measured before etching and after 5 and 25 cycles for each chemistry. AES corroborates ellipsometry: for BCl₃, Zr and O drop rapidly and B residues appear; for SOCl₂, S signal is detectable and Zr/O remain high (sulfate formation and poor removal); for TiCl₄, Zr decreases moderately while Cl/W signatures indicate incomplete volatile conversion. Together the panels link kinetics (how quickly ZrO₂ is removed) with surface chemistry (what residues form), explaining why BCl₃ is optimal for ZrO₂ ALE and SOCl₂ fails.

AES corroborates ellipsometry: for BCl₃, Zr and O drop rapidly and B residues appear; for SOCl₂, S signal is detectable and Zr/O remain high (sulfate formation and poor removal); for TiCl₄, Zr decreases moderately while Cl/W signatures indicate incomplete volatile conversion. Together the panels link kinetics (how quickly ZrO₂ is removed) with surface chemistry (what residues form), explaining why BCl₃ is optimal for ZrO₂ ALE and SOCl₂ fails.

d.question: How can the combined ellipsometry and AES results guide the choice of co-reactant for ZrO₂ ALE?
d.answer: The ellipsometry data show that BCl₃ and TiCl₄ enable rapid, self-limiting ZrO₂ removal, whereas SOCl₂ fails due to slow kinetics. AES confirms this by revealing B residues for BCl₃ and Cl/W residues for TiCl₄, but heavy S residues and persistent Zr/O for SOCl₂. Therefore, WF₆/BCl₃ is the most efficient chemistry for ZrO₂ ALE, while SOCl₂ should be avoided because it forms nonvolatile sulfates.

a.question: Does Figure 8 show that different co-reactants produce significantly different ZrO₂ etch rates during ALE?
a.answer: Yes. WF₆/BCl₃ and WF₆/TiCl₄ rapidly remove ZrO₂ within a few cycles, while WF₆/SOCl₂ barely etches the film even after 25 cycles.

c.question: Which surface residues observed by AES explain poor etching performance for WF₆/SOCl₂?
c.answer: Sulfur-containing species (S), Persistent Zr and O (indicating unremoved oxide), Minimal exposure of the Si substrate, These suggest sulfate formation, which is nonvolatile and blocks etching.

b.question: Which co-reactant removes ZrO₂ the fastest according to the ellipsometry curves?
b.answer: WF₆/BCl₃ yields the fastest ZrO₂ removal, with the film almost completely etched in about 3 cycles.
